---
title: "Figure 13 Diblock-Star Chain"
subtitle: "Reproduce a published hierarchical SEB structure"
jupyter: python3
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: show
    code-tools: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  error: false
---

This tutorial constructs the hierarchy used for Figure 13 of the original SEB
formalism: a diblock copolymer, a four-arm star made from that diblock, and a
linear chain containing five instances of the star. The final structure has 40
Gaussian-polymer subunits but only four independent physical parameters.

Read [Reusable Nested Structures](nested-structures.qmd) first if nested
reference paths are unfamiliar. Publication details are collected in the
[citation guide](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki/Publications-and-Citation).

## Structure hierarchy

```text
diblock:       A ---- B

four-arm star:     A--B
                    |
                B--A--B
                    |
                   A--B

star chain:       --*-- = --*-- = --*-- = --*-- = --*--
                    |       |       |       |       |
```

The graphs are defined once and instantiated repeatedly. Internal names are
therefore shared across every instance.

## Python

### 1. Define the reusable diblock

Create one `A--B` graph by attaching the first end of B to the second end of A.

```{python}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pyseb

world = pyseb.World("Figure13Tutorial")

diblock = world.Add("GaussianPolymer", "A")
world.Link("GaussianPolymer", "B.end1", "A.end2")
```

The graph identifier `diblock` can now be instantiated inside a larger graph.

### 2. Assemble a four-arm star

Create the first diblock instance, then connect three more instances at their
`A.end1` references. All four A blocks meet at the star center.

```{python}
star = world.Add(diblock, "diblock1")
world.Link(diblock, "diblock2:A.end1", "diblock1:A.end1")
world.Link(diblock, "diblock3:A.end1", "diblock1:A.end1")
world.Link(diblock, "diblock4:A.end1", "diblock1:A.end1")
```

Each free `B.end2` reference lies at the outer end of one star arm.

### 3. Link five star instances

Join a free B end on each new star to a free B end on the preceding star. The
colon-separated paths descend through the star, diblock, and polymer levels.

```{python}
chain = world.Add(star, "star1")
world.Link(
    star,
    "star2:diblock1:B.end2",
    "star1:diblock3:B.end2",
)
world.Link(
    star,
    "star3:diblock1:B.end2",
    "star2:diblock3:B.end2",
)
world.Link(
    star,
    "star4:diblock1:B.end2",
    "star3:diblock3:B.end2",
)
world.Link(
    star,
    "star5:diblock1:B.end2",
    "star4:diblock3:B.end2",
)
world.Add(chain, "chain")
```

The named `chain` contains five stars, twenty diblocks, and forty Gaussian
polymers.

### 4. Reuse the shared parameters

Every diblock instance retains the internal names A and B. Consequently all A
blocks share one `beta` and `Rg`, and all B blocks share another pair.

```{python}
parameters = {
    "beta_A": 1.0,
    "beta_B": 1.0,
    "Rg_A": 1.0,
    "Rg_B": 1.0,
}

print("physical parameters:", sorted(parameters))
print("subunits: 5 stars x 4 diblocks x 2 polymers = 40")
```

Changing these four values updates all twenty diblock instances together.

### 5. Evaluate and plot the form factor

Direct numerical traversal evaluates the hierarchical model over a logarithmic
q grid without first expanding the full symbolic expression.

```{python}
q = np.geomspace(1e-2, 50.0, 320)
form_factor = np.asarray(
    world.EvaluateFormFactor("chain", parameters, q.tolist())
)

for index in (0, 80, 160, 240, 319):
    print(f"q={q[index]:.5g}, F(q)={form_factor[index]:.8g}")
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-figure13-star-chain
#| fig-cap: "Normalized form factor of the five-star diblock chain."

plt.loglog(q, form_factor)
plt.xlabel(r"$q$")
plt.ylabel(r"$F(q)$")
plt.show()
```

The multiple shoulders and changes of slope reflect the hierarchy of polymer,
star, and chain length scales.

## C++

The maintained C++14 program follows the same three-level construction and
evaluates representative q values with the portable backend.

```cpp
{{< include cpp/figure13-star-chain.cpp >}}
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
from _helpers import run_tutorial

run_tutorial("figure13-star-chain")
```

## Next steps

- [Review reusable nested structures](nested-structures.qmd)
- [Theory overview](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki/Theory-Overview)
- [Complete maintained Python source](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/blob/main/pyseb/examples/python/figure13_star_chain.py)
- [Original C++ example](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/blob/main/Examples/DiBlockStarChain.cpp)
